target.name: (+)-Mangicol D
target.motivation: Mangicols are a new class of sesterterpenoids featuring unprecedented spirotetracyclic cores and notable bioactivities, including cytotoxicity and anti-inflammatory activity. The stereochemistry of the flexible polyol side chain was challenging to determine and required revision.

prior_syntheses.key_strategy: Enantioselective synthesis of the spirotetracyclic carbon core using a stereoselective transannular Diels−Alder strategy.
Synthesis of the tetracyclic core of the neomangicols using a late-stage indene alkylation.
Rh(I)-catalyzed tandem Pauson−Khand/[4+2] cycloaddition for the spirotricyclic core analogue.
Asymmetric approach to the multicyclic core structure of Mangicol A.

success_criteria.free_text: First asymmetric total synthesis of mangicol D, leading to a structural revision of its polyol side chain. The final product's NMR data should match natural mangicol D and its triacetate derivative should yield crystals suitable for X-ray diffraction.

key_disconnections.reasoning: Decouples the tetracyclic core from the side chain, enabling late-stage variation of side chain configuration for stereochemical validation.
To close the remaining five-membered ring of the terpene core and establish the spiro-fused tetracyclic framework.
To establish the final quaternary center of the core.
To introduce an alkynyl chain onto the six-membered ring.
To establish the quaternary center with excellent stereocontrol and form the A-ring.

retrosynthetic_strategy.rationale: The synthesis aimed to decouple the tetracyclic core from the polyol side chain to allow for late-stage stereochemical validation. The A/B ring system, conserved across the mangicol family, was targeted using a 5,6-fused bicyclic enone as a key building block. Key reactions included a domino carbopalladation-carbonylation (Heck cyclization), Conia-ene cyclization, and SmI2-mediated reductive cyclization to efficiently forge the spirotetracyclic core. The enone functionality was exploited for C-C bond formation via one- and two-electron processes, and the cis-hydrindane framework for stereocontrolled assembly. The strategy was designed to allow for the synthesis of different side chain diastereomers to determine the correct stereochemistry of mangicol D.

thought.background: Starting from known enantioenriched diketone 14, the goal is to introduce an isopropenyl unit and a vinyl triflate for subsequent A-ring assembly.
thought.gap: How to selectively modify the two ketone functionalities in diketone 14 and prepare a vinyl triflate.
thought.decision: To selectively convert the methyl ketone into an isopropenyl unit, chose Wittig olefination. To prepare the vinyl triflate, chose triflation of the remaining cyclohexanone with KHMDS and Comins’ reagent.

outcome.observation: Selective Wittig olefination and subsequent triflation successfully yielded vinyl triflate 15. (63% for Wittig, 90% for triflation, overall 56.7%)

evidence.text_span: Our synthesis commenced from known enantioenriched diketone 14, which is prepared on a multigram scale from commercial materials in a one-pot (two-step) procedure with a single purification (Scheme 1).8 A selective Wittig olefination converted the methyl ketone into an isopropenyl unit, and the remaining cyclohexanone was triflated (KHMDS, Comins’ reagent) to generate vinyl triflate 15, thus setting the stage for A-ring assembly.

thought.background: Vinyl triflate 15 is ready for A-ring assembly.
thought.gap: How to form the A-ring and introduce a handle for late-stage polyol side chain elaboration.
thought.decision: To establish the quaternary center with stereocontrol and introduce a methyl ester, chose Heck cyclization followed by trapping of the C(sp3)-Pd(II) intermediate with CO/MeOH.

outcome.observation: Heck cyclization established the quaternary center with excellent stereocontrol, and carbonylation afforded methyl ester 16.

evidence.text_span: Heck cyclization established the quaternary center with excellent stereocontrol,9−11 and trapping of the ensuing C(sp3)-Pd(II) intermediate with CO/MeOH afforded methyl ester 16, providing a handle for late-stage polyol side chain elaboration.12

thought.background: Methyl ester 16 contains a cyclic ketal and an unconjugated olefin.
thought.gap: How to remove the cyclic ketal and isomerize the olefin to a conjugated enone.
thought.decision: To remove the cyclic ketal and promote olefin migration, chose BF3·OEt2 treatment.

outcome.observation: Removal of the cyclic ketal with BF3·OEt2 also promoted concomitant olefin migration to yield conjugated enone 17 as the major alkene isomer in a single step.

evidence.text_span: Removal of the cyclic ketal with BF3·OEt2 also promoted concomitant olefin migration to yield conjugated enone 17 as the major alkene isomer in a single step.

thought.background: Enone 17 is available, and the next step is to introduce alkynyl chains.
thought.gap: How to introduce a handle for cross-coupling on the enone.
thought.decision: To provide a handle for cross-coupling, chose α-iodination of the enone.

outcome.observation: α-Iodination of the enone provided 18, a handle for cross-coupling.

evidence.text_span: With straightforward access to enone 17, we next proceeded to introduce the two alkynyl chains onto the six-membered ring. α-Iodination of the enone provided a handle for crosscoupling, which was accomplished via a Negishi coupling with Grignard 20 (ZnCl2, Pd(t·Bu3P)2).

thought.background: Iodinated enone 18 is ready for the first alkynyl chain introduction.
thought.gap: How to introduce the first alkynyl chain via cross-coupling.
thought.decision: To introduce the alkynyl chain, chose Negishi coupling with Grignard 20.

outcome.observation: Negishi coupling successfully introduced the first alkynyl chain, yielding 19.

evidence.text_span: α-Iodination of the enone provided a handle for crosscoupling, which was accomplished via a Negishi coupling with Grignard 20 (ZnCl2, Pd(t·Bu3P)2).

thought.background: Compound 19 has one alkynyl chain and an enone.
thought.gap: How to introduce the second alkynyl chain and form a silyl enol ether.
thought.decision: To introduce the second alkynyl chain and form a silyl enol ether, chose 1,4-addition of a cuprate derived from 20, followed by TMSCl trapping.

outcome.observation: Diastereoselective 1,4-addition to 19 from the convex face furnished 21 in high yield.

evidence.text_span: Organometallic 20 was also employed in the next key C−C bond-forming step by conversion to a cuprate and diastereoselective 1,4-addition to 19 from the convex face, furnishing 21 in high yield.

thought.background: Compound 21 has a latent carbonyl and an appended alkyne.
thought.gap: How to establish the final quaternary center of the core via cyclization.
thought.decision: To establish the final quaternary center, chose a Conia-ene cyclization under mild basic conditions (t-BuOK/DMSO).

outcome.observation: The desired C-C bond was formed in 63% yield, presumably via initial alkoxide-mediated cleavage of the silyl enol ether to generate an enolate. Both alkynyl TMS groups were also concomitantly removed.

evidence.text_span: With a latent carbonyl and an appended alkyne in place, a Conia-ene cyclization was leveraged to establish the final quaternary center of the core.13 Employing Trauner’s mild conditions (t-BuOK/DMSO, room temperature), the desired C−C bond was formed in 63% yield,14 presumably via initial alkoxidemediated cleavage of the silyl enol ether to generate an enolate.15,16 Notably, both alkynyl TMS groups were also concomitantly removed under these basic conditions.

thought.background: Compound 22 has the core with a latent carbonyl and an alkyne.
thought.gap: How to close the remaining five-membered ring of the terpene core.
thought.decision: To close the five-membered ring, chose a SmI2/H2O-mediated carbonyl-alkyne reductive cyclization.

outcome.observation: The closure of the remaining five-membered ring of the terpene core was achieved through a SmI2/H2O-mediated carbonyl-alkyne reductive cyclization and delivered the tetracyclic core (see 23) in excellent yield.

evidence.text_span: The closure of the remaining five-membered ring of the terpene core was achieved through a SmI2/H2O-mediated carbonylalkyne reductive cyclization and delivered the tetracyclic core (see 23) in excellent yield.17,18

thought.background: The SmI2/H2O-mediated cyclization delivered the tetracyclic core 23.
thought.gap: The use of an alkene (rather than alkyne) coupling partner could increase efficiency, but the stereochemical outcome needs to be confirmed.
thought.decision: To explore an alkene coupling partner for increased efficiency, but this strategy was found to afford cyclized material with the incorrect stereochemical configuration at C9.

outcome.observation: The use of an alkene (rather than alkyne) coupling partner would increase the efficiency of the synthesis, our SmI2/H2O-mediated conditions afforded cyclized material with the incorrect stereochemical configuration at C9 when this strategy was explored.

evidence.text_span: While the use of an alkene (rather than alkyne) coupling partner would increase the efficiency of the synthesis, our SmI2/H2O-mediated conditions afforded cyclized material with the incorrect stereochemical configuration at C9 when this strategy was explored.19

thought.background: The SmI2/H2O-mediated cyclization with an alkene coupling partner resulted in incorrect stereochemistry at C9.
thought.gap: Need to ensure correct stereochemistry during the cyclization.
thought.decision: The original alkyne coupling partner strategy was maintained, as it provided the correct stereochemistry for the tetracyclic core.

outcome.observation: The alkyne coupling partner strategy was confirmed to be the correct approach for the SmI2-mediated cyclization.

evidence.text_span: While the use of an alkene (rather than alkyne) coupling partner would increase the efficiency of the synthesis, our SmI2/H2O-mediated conditions afforded cyclized material with the incorrect stereochemical configuration at C9 when this strategy was explored.19

thought.background: Tetracyclic core 23 has two alkenes that need to be reduced, and the newly formed methyl stereocenters need to be controlled.
thought.gap: How to stereoselectively hydrogenate both alkenes and control the diastereoselectivity at the methyl stereocenters.
thought.decision: To achieve stereoselective hydrogenation, chose to place a bulky TMS group on the tertiary alcohol before hydrogenation with Adams’ catalyst.

outcome.observation: When a bulky TMS group was placed on the tertiary alcohol, a completely stereoselective hydrogenation occurred, giving ester 24 after concomitant desilylation by the alcohol solvent. (98% for silylation, 86% for hydrogenation, overall 84.28%)

evidence.text_span: Hydrogenation of 23 with Adams’ catalyst reduced both alkenes but provided a mixture of diastereomers at the newly formed methyl stereocenters.20 When a bulky TMS group was placed on the tertiary alcohol, however, a completely stereoselective hydrogenation occurred, giving ester 24 after concomitant desilylation by the alcohol solvent.

thought.background: Ester 24 has a tertiary alcohol that needs to be dehydrated to form a trisubstituted alkene.
thought.gap: How to selectively dehydrate the tertiary alcohol to form the desired trisubstituted alkene without forming the tetrasubstituted isomer exclusively.
thought.decision: To achieve the desired trisubstituted alkene, chose Burgess reagent for dehydration.

outcome.observation: Burgess dehydration of the tertiary alcohol furnished the trisubstituted alkene along with minor amounts of the tetrasubstituted isomer (3.1:1 ratio).

evidence.text_span: Burgess dehydration of the tertiary alcohol then furnished the trisubstituted alkene along with minor amounts of the tetrasubstituted isomer (3.1:1 ratio) likely due to an unusually facile E1 elimination reaction in this scaffold.

thought.background: Dehydration of the tertiary alcohol in 24 to form the trisubstituted alkene.
thought.gap: Other dehydration reagents might lead to different regioselectivity.
thought.decision: To explore MsCl/Et3N and Martin’s sulfurane for dehydration.

outcome.observation: Both MsCl/Et3N and Martin’s sulfurane afforded the tetrasubstituted alkene isomer exclusively, which was not the desired product.

evidence.text_span: Notably, both MsCl/Et3N and Martin’s sulfurane afforded the tetrasubstituted alkene isomer exclusively.

thought.background: MsCl/Et3N and Martin’s sulfurane exclusively yielded the tetrasubstituted alkene.
thought.gap: Need a reagent that favors the trisubstituted alkene.
thought.decision: Burgess reagent was chosen as it provided the desired trisubstituted alkene as the major product.

outcome.observation: Burgess reagent was confirmed as the suitable reagent for selective dehydration to the trisubstituted alkene.

evidence.text_span: Burgess dehydration of the tertiary alcohol then furnished the trisubstituted alkene along with minor amounts of the tetrasubstituted isomer (3.1:1 ratio) likely due to an unusually facile E1 elimination reaction in this scaffold. Notably, both MsCl/Et3N and Martin’s sulfurane afforded the tetrasubstituted alkene isomer exclusively.

thought.background: The core structure is complete, and the methyl ester needs to be converted to an aldehyde for side chain introduction.
thought.gap: How to reduce the methyl ester to an aldehyde.
thought.decision: To reduce the methyl ester to an aldehyde, chose DIBAL reduction.

outcome.observation: DIBAL reduction of the methyl ester generated aldehyde 25 in high yield.

evidence.text_span: Finally, a DIBAL reduction of the methyl ester generated aldehyde 25, setting the stage for introduction of the final polyol side chain.

thought.background: Aldehyde 25 is ready for the introduction of the polyol side chain. The proposed structure of mangicol D suggests an (E)-configured vinyl iodide for the terminal C4 fragment.
thought.gap: How to introduce the side chain and what stereochemical outcome to expect at C17.
thought.decision: To introduce the side chain, chose lithium-halogen exchange of (E)-configured vinyl iodide 27 followed by addition to aldehyde 25.

outcome.observation: Lithium−halogen exchange of 27 generated the corresponding vinyl lithium, which was added to aldehyde 25 to give a 1:1 mixture of C17 diastereomeric alcohols (26 + epi-26) in 60% yield.

evidence.text_span: (E)-Configured vinyl iodide 27 was selected as the terminal C4 fragment based on the proposed structure of mangicol D and a planned cis-dihydroxylation reaction (Scheme 1).21 Lithium−halogen exchange of 27 generated the corresponding vinyl lithium, which was added to aldehyde 25 to give a 1:1 mixture of C17 diastereomeric alcohols (26 + epi-26) in 60% yield.

thought.background: The C17 diastereomeric alcohols (26 + epi-26) were obtained.
thought.gap: How to perform cis-dihydroxylation and deprotect the PMB group to complete the polyol motif.
thought.decision: To perform cis-dihydroxylation, chose standard conditions (OsO4, NMO, acetone/H2O). For PMB deprotection, chose Pd/C and H2.

outcome.observation: Standard cis-dihydroxylation conditions proceeded with low diastereoselectivity (~1.5:1−2:1 dr) for both isomers, affording access to four of the eight possible diastereomers of the side-chain region. A final PMB deprotection then completed assembly of the polyol motif. Overall yield 22-45% over two steps, average 33.75%.

evidence.text_span: After separation, each diastereomer was subjected to standard cis-dihydroxylation conditions (OsO4, NMO, acetone/H2O), which proceeded with low diastereoselectivity (~1.5:1−2:1 dr) for both isomers and thus afforded access to four of the eight possible diastereomers of the side-chain region (see Figure 1). A final PMB deprotection then completed assembly of the polyol motif. In accordance with Fenical’s studies during the structure elucidation of the mangicols,3 it was not possible to unambiguously determine the structures of these four diastereomers (either PMBprotected or as free triols) by NMR analysis. However, all four isomers of 6 showed significant discrepancies relative to the reported NMR data for mangicol D, particularly in the C16−C22 region (see SI), suggesting a stereochemical misassignment of the polyol side chain.

thought.background: Four diastereomers of mangicol D were synthesized.
thought.gap: Unambiguous determination of the structures of these four diastereomers by NMR analysis was not possible, and they showed discrepancies with reported NMR data for mangicol D.
thought.decision: To compare the synthesized isomers with reported NMR data and conclude a potential stereochemical misassignment.

outcome.observation: It was not possible to unambiguously determine the structures of these four diastereomers by NMR analysis. All four isomers of 6 showed significant discrepancies relative to the reported NMR data for mangicol D, particularly in the C16−C22 region, suggesting a stereochemical misassignment of the polyol side chain.

evidence.text_span: In accordance with Fenical’s studies during the structure elucidation of the mangicols,3 it was not possible to unambiguously determine the structures of these four diastereomers (either PMBprotected or as free triols) by NMR analysis. However, all four isomers of 6 showed significant discrepancies relative to the reported NMR data for mangicol D, particularly in the C16−C22 region (see SI), suggesting a stereochemical misassignment of the polyol side chain.

thought.background: The previous synthesis attempts yielded diastereomers that did not match the reported NMR data for mangicol D, suggesting a stereochemical misassignment.
thought.gap: Need to determine the true stereochemistry of mangicol D's polyol side chain.
thought.decision: To review recent literature, specifically a disclosure of mangicol M (28) with confirmed X-ray diffraction structure, which possesses 17R, 18R, 19S stereochemistry, and the re-isolation of mangicol D with matching NMR data.

outcome.observation: A recent disclosure of mangicol M (28) with confirmed X-ray diffraction (17R, 18R, 19S stereochemistry) and re-isolation of mangicol D provided a hypothesis for the correct stereochemistry (17R, 18R) of the polyol side chain.

evidence.text_span: Determination of the true structure of 6 was aided by a recent disclosure of eight additional mangicol members derived from heterologous expression of a Fusarium oxysporum 14005 biosynthetic gene cluster.22 In particular, a THF-ringcontaining member, mangicol M (28), was isolated under these conditions, and its structure was confirmed by singlecrystal X-ray diffraction (Scheme 2). Notably, mangicol M possesses 17R, 18R, 19S stereochemistry. In addition, mangicol D was also reisolated alongside 28, and its reported NMR data closely matched Fenical’s original isolate. Hypothesizing that the mangicols possess conserved stereochemistry along the polyol side chain, we therefore targeted the 17R, 18R diastereomer for synthesis.

thought.background: Based on the literature review, the 17R, 18R diastereomer is targeted. This requires a (Z)-configured vinyl iodide.
thought.gap: How to introduce the (Z)-configured side chain and what stereochemical outcome to expect at C17.
thought.decision: To introduce the (Z)-configured side chain, chose lithium-halogen exchange of (Z)-configured vinyl iodide 29 followed by addition to aldehyde 25.

outcome.observation: (Z)-Configured vinyl iodide 29 was prepared, and its lithiate added to 25 to generate 30 and its C17 epimer in high yield, again as a 1:1 mixture of separable diastereomers.

evidence.text_span: (Z)-Configured vinyl iodide 29 was prepared, and its lithiate added to 25 to generate 30 and its C17 epimer in high yield, again as a 1:1 mixture of separable diastereomers (Scheme 2 .23

thought.background: Compound 30 (one of the C17 diastereomers) is available.
thought.gap: How to perform asymmetric dihydroxylation to achieve the desired 17R, 18R stereochemistry.
thought.decision: To achieve asymmetric dihydroxylation, chose Sharpless asymmetric dihydroxylation with (DHQD)2PHAL as the chiral ligand.

outcome.observation: Subjecting 30 to Sharpless asymmetric dihydroxylation with (DHQD)2PHAL as the chiral ligand afforded 31 in 78% yield.

evidence.text_span: Subjecting 30 to Sharpless asymmetric dihydroxylation with (DHQD)2PHAL as the chiral ligand afforded 31 in 78% yield.

thought.background: Compound 31 contains a PMB protecting group.
thought.gap: How to deprotect the PMB group to obtain the final mangicol D structure.
thought.decision: To deprotect the PMB group, chose hydrogenation with Pd/C and H2.

outcome.observation: Subsequent deprotection of the PMB group furnished 6a in 95% yield. The NMR spectroscopic data of 6a were in complete accordance with natural mangicol D.

evidence.text_span: Subsequent deprotection of the PMB group (Pd/C, H2, MeOH, Δ) furnished 6a in 95% yield. The NMR spectroscopic data of 6a were in complete accordance with natural mangicol D reported by both the Fenical and Liu and Jiang laboratories.3,22

thought.background: The synthesized mangicol D (6a) matched reported NMR data.
thought.gap: Need unambiguous assignment of the side chain stereochemistry.
thought.decision: To achieve unambiguous assignment, chose acetylation of 6a and subsequent crystallization of the resulting triacetate derivative 32 for X-ray diffraction.

outcome.observation: Acetylation of 6a and subsequent crystallization of the resulting triacetate derivative 32 yielded crystals suitable for X-ray diffraction, confirming the side chain stereochemistry.

evidence.text_span: Despite this match, unambiguous assignment of the side chain stereochemistry was achieved by acetylation of 6a (Ac2O, DMAP, Et3N,) and subsequent crystallization of the resulting triacetate derivative 32. Notably, compound 32 was the only derivative that yielded crystals suitable for X-ray diffraction among all the diastereomers prepared in this study, as well as a variety of derivatized products.

Overall Yield.interpretation: Not explicitly stated as a single overall yield, but individual step yields are provided.

Longest Linear Steps.interpretation: The described route proceeded in 16 steps from 14.

Total Steps.interpretation: The described route proceeded in 16 steps from 14.

Failed Attempts Count.interpretation: Two failed attempts were explicitly mentioned and revised.

validation.comparison_to_prior: The NMR spectroscopic data of the synthesized mangicol D (6a) were in complete accordance with natural mangicol D reported by both the Fenical and Liu and Jiang laboratories. The initial synthesis attempts yielded diastereomers that showed significant discrepancies relative to the reported NMR data, leading to a structural revision.
validation.significance: This is the first asymmetric total synthesis of mangicol D. The synthesis led to a structural revision of the polyol side chain of mangicol D, confirming the 17R, 18R configuration. This finding has implications for the stereochemical assignment of other mangicol and neomangicol structures, which were previously assigned by analogy. The route is efficient (16 steps from 14) and scalable (over 300 mg of 24 prepared), boding well for future syntheses and medicinal chemistry efforts.

source.title: Asymmetric Total Synthesis and Structure Revision of (+)-Mangicol D.
source.authors: Xie Y, Wang Y, Zhu Z, Ning S, Maimone TJ.
